perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall not disclose Confidential Information to any of its Representatives or Representatives of its Affiliates, except as required to carry out the Purpose under this Agreement for the benefit of Deere and its Affiliates. “Representative” shall mean any officer, director, employee, contractor, agent, supplier, or advisor of its respective entity. “Affiliate” shall mean any corporation, partnership, or other entity that, either directly or indirectly, controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with a party, where control is defined as having more than a fifty percent (50%) controlling inter est.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall not disclose Confidential Information to any of its Representatives or Representatives of its Affiliates, except as required to carry out the Purpose under this Agreement. “Representative” shall mean any officer, director, employee, contractor, agent, or advisor of its respective entity. “Affiliate” shall mean any corporation, partnership, or other entity that, either directly or indirectly, controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with a party, where control is defined as having more than a twenty-five percent (25%) controlling interest.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Affiliate' has been changed from a 50% controlling interest to a 25% controlling interest. This is a significant change that introduces potential conflict as it deviates from the standard legal definition used in many jurisdictions.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition of 'Affiliate' contradicts the standard definition used by the SEC in Rule 12b-2 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, which typically looks at control exceeding 50% ownership. Lowering the threshold to 25% can create uncertainty about who is considered an affiliate for disclosure and compliance purposes.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Affiliate' from a 50% controlling interest to a 25% controlling interest. The Law Explanation states that this contradicts the standard definition used by the SEC in Rule 12b-2, which typically considers control exceeding 50% ownership. Snippet 2 references 17 CFR § 240.12b-2, which provides definitions related to securities regulations. The change creates a legal contradiction by deviating from a legally recognized definition, potentially making the contract unenforceable. The Perturbation Explanation supports this as it introduces potential conflict as it deviates from the standard legal definition used in many jurisdictions. Therefore, because the definition has been altered to mean something different than what is defined under federal regulations, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: All forms of Confidential Information, tangible and electronic, and all copies thereof, shall be and remain the sole and exclusive property of Discloser, and shall be promptly returned or destroyed by Recipient upon the earlier of the written request of Discloser or the termination of this Agreement. Except as noted in the immediately following sentence, Confidential Information contained in electronically archived notes, reports, correspondences, emails, and other business records prepared by Recipient in connection with the Purpose under this Agreement need not be returned or destroyed, but shall be subject to all other terms of this Agreement. Confidential Information in the form of schematics, prints, drawings, formulations, and descriptions of processes and know -how, and the like, must be returned to Discloser or destroyed pursuant to this Section 6.
perturbation.changed_text: All forms of Confidential Information, tangible and electronic, and all copies thereof, shall be and remain the sole and exclusive property of Discloser, and shall be promptly returned or destroyed by Recipient upon the termination of this Agreement, regardless of whether Discloser requests it. Except as noted in the immediately following sentence, Confidential Information contained in electronically archived notes, reports, correspondences, emails, and other business records prepared by Recipient in connection with the Purpose under this Agreement need not be returned or destroyed, and shall be subject to only some terms of this Agreement. Confidential Information in the form of schematics, prints, drawings, formulations, and descriptions of processes and know -how, and the like, does not have to be returned to Discloser or destroyed pursuant to this Section 6.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the Discloser's ability to request a return of confidential information prior to the termination of the agreement, contradicting standard confidentiality practices, and creates ambiguity by stating only some terms of the agreement apply.
perturbation.law_explanation: By limiting the Discloser's right to retrieve confidential information, the modified text weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets under the UTSA. The UTSA emphasizes preventing unauthorized use and disclosure, and restricting the ability to reclaim information hinders this protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the protection of confidential information, moving from a requirement to return or destroy information upon request to only upon termination, and eliminating the obligation to return schematics, prints, drawings, formulations, and descriptions of processes and know -how, and the like. The Law Explanation states the UTSA emphasizes preventing unauthorized use and disclosure. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the removal of the Discloser's rights and the explicit statement that schematics, prints, drawings, formulations, and descriptions of processes and know -how, and the like do not have to be returned significantly undermines the protection of confidential information as described in the Law Explanation, thus creating a legal contradiction under the definition of Misaligned Terminology.

perturbation.original_text: ALL CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION UNDER THIS AGREEMENT IS PROVIDED TO RECIPIENT “AS- IS” WITHOUT ANY REPRESENTATIONS OR WARRANTIES, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, AS TO ITS ACCURACY, COMPLETENESS OR PERFORMANCE.
perturbation.changed_text: ALL CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION UNDER THIS AGREEMENT IS PROVIDED TO RECIPIENT WITH A WARRANTY OF MERCHANTABILITY.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a warranty of merchantability to the confidential information, which is contradictory to the typical 'AS-IS' disclaimer in NDAs. This creates ambiguity and potential liability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties
perturbation.law_explanation: By providing a warranty of merchantability, the text implies that the confidential information is fit for its ordinary purpose, contradicting the common practice of providing confidential information 'as-is' without any warranties. This creates uncertainty regarding the quality and reliability of the information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a warranty of merchantability, directly contradicting the original 'AS-IS' disclaimer for confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly subsection (3)(a), states that "unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like 'as is', 'with all faults' or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty." By replacing the 'AS-IS' clause with a warranty, the modified text creates a clear contradiction, as it now implies a warranty where there was explicitly none before, potentially leading to legal disputes over the quality or reliability of the confidential information. This falls under the category of misaligned terminology leading to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The laws of the State of Iowa , without giving effect to its conflicts of law principles, govern all matters arising out of or relating to t his Agreement. The parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of, and forum and/or venue in, any federal or state court of competent jurisdiction located in Iowa for the purposes of adjudicating any matter arising out of or relating to this Agreement. Litigation or legal proceedings which arise out of or relate to this Agreement are to be conducted before a judge and not a jury.
perturbation.changed_text: The laws of the State of Iowa , without giving effect to its conflicts of law principles, govern all matters arising out of or relating to this Agreement. The parties consent to the jurisdiction of a court not located in Iowa for adjudicating any matter arising out of or relating to this Agreement. Litigation or legal proceedings which arise out of or relate to this Agreement are to be conducted before a jury.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the agreement to allow for a different jurisdiction and mandates a jury trial. This creates ambiguity in where a case would be held, removing the forum selection, and removes the waiver of the right to a jury trial.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the exclusive jurisdiction clause in the contract, it allows for a potential change of venue based on 28 U.S. Code § 1404, which permits a district court to transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought. This contradicts the original intent to have all matters adjudicated in Iowa and opens the door for litigation in a potentially less favorable jurisdiction.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the original contract by removing the exclusive jurisdiction clause, permitting litigation outside of Iowa, and mandating a jury trial, which was initially waived. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, both citing 28 U.S. Code § 1404, confirm that a district court can transfer a civil action to another district. The removal of the exclusive jurisdiction and venue in Iowa opens the door for this transfer, directly contradicting the original intent of the agreement to adjudicate matters solely in Iowa. By mandating a jury trial, this also contradicts the initial waiver.
